questions.question: What is hypoxia?

options.a: A condition where the cells of the body cannot absorb enough carbon dioxide to function properly
options.b: A condition where the cells of the body burst due to lack of oxygen
options.c: A condition where the cells of the body do not receive sufficient oxygen to function properly

questions.question: A sensory illusion is where our brain interprets sensory information and tells us our body is moving in ways it is not. Which is NOT a type of Sensory Illusion?

options.a: on the downwind leg at circuit height.
options.b: from the upwind side of the runway in all cases.
options.c: on base leg if convenient.
options.d: on final for a straight in approach.

questions.question: When a NORDO aircraft crosses an airport for the purpose of obtaining landing information it should maintain

options.a: circuit height.
options.b: 1,000 feet above circuit height.
options.c: at least 2,000 feet AGL.
options.d: at least 500 feet above circuit height.

questions.question: An aircraft is “cleared to the circuit” where a left hand circuit is in effect. Without further approval from ATC a right turn may be made to

options.a: join the final leg.
options.b: join the base leg.
options.c: join crosswind or a partial right turn to join the downwind leg.
options.d: descend on the downwind leg.

questions.question: When instructed to continue an approach to a runway which is clear of traffic, what action should the pilot take if no landing clearance is received?

options.a: Circle 360° to the left.
options.b: Circle 360° in the direction of the circuit.
options.c: Complete the landing.
options.d: Request landing clearance.

questions.question: A NOTAM has been published for an airport, which is 400 feet ASL, stating the circuit height is 1,500 feet ASL. When the ceiling is 1,000 overcast and the visibility is 3 miles, the circuit height in controlled airspace should be

options.a: 500 feet below the cloud base.
options.b: 1,500 feet ASL.
options.c: 1,100 feet above the airport elevation.
options.d: 1,000 feet above the airport elevation.

questions.question: When the reported ceiling is 1,000 feet overcast and visibility is 3 miles, to remain VFR, an aircraft cleared to the circuit must join

options.a: as high as possible without entering cloud.
options.b: at 500 feet below cloud base.
options.c: at 700 feet AGL.
options.d: in accordance with Special VFR.

questions.question: Aircraft flying VFR normally join the circuit at 1,000 feet AAE. This may not always be possible because of

options.a: the possibility of a “straight in” clearance to the airport in which case the final leg would normally be entered at less than 1,000 feet.
options.b: the existence of a NOTAM which provides for a different circuit altitude.
options.c: weather conditions which may necessitate a circuit height lower than 1,000 feet.
options.d: the existence of any of the above circumstances.

questions.question: A pilot on final approach is requested by ATC to reduce airspeed. The pilot should

options.a: comply, giving due consideration to safe minimum manoeuvring speed of the aircraft.
options.b: acknowledge transmission and execute a 360° turn.
options.c: overshoot and rejoin the circuit.
options.d: reduce airspeed well below normal approach speed range.

questions.question: A pilot is cleared to land but is concerned about the high crosswind component. The pilot should

options.a: use full flaps and approach at a reduced speed.
options.b: alter heading and land on another runway which is more into wind.
options.c: overshoot and request an into-wind runway.
options.d: continue the approach and land as the clearance must be obeyed.

questions.question: A pilot on a VFR flight is being vectored by ATC towards an extensive unbroken layer of cloud. The responsibility for remaining VFR rests with

options.a: the radar operator.
options.b: ATC since the flight is designated VFR.
options.c: ATC since the cloud is visible on radar.
options.d: the pilot.

questions.question: A student pilot on a VFR flight has been given a radar vector by ATC. Ahead, at a lower altitude, is a solid overcast cloud condition. The pilot should

options.a: climb above the cloud and fly “VFR over the top”.
options.b: alter heading as necessary to remain VFR and advise ATC.
options.c: maintain heading and altitude as it is an ATC clearance.
options.d: maintain heading and altitude because ATC knows of the cloud and will issue further instructions.

questions.question: An aircraft on a Special VFR flight has been cleared for a “straight in” approach. Because of low ceiling and poor visibility, the pilot is concerned about the exact location of a radio mast in the vicinity. Avoiding this obstruction is the responsibility

options.a: of the pilot.
options.b: of the tower controller as the controller is aware of the obstruction.
options.c: of ATC as the pilot has been given Special VFR clearance.
options.d: shared equally by the pilot and the controller.

questions.question: A pilot on a Special VFR flight has been cleared to the circuit. Ahead, at a lower altitude, is a solid layer of stratus cloud. Remaining clear of cloud is the responsibility of

options.a: the tower controller as it is within a Control Zone.
options.b: ATC because the weather is below VFR.
options.c: the pilot and ATC.
options.d: the pilot.

questions.question: A pilot on a VFR flight in Class C airspace is advised by ATC to maintain a specific heading. In the pilot’s opinion, this heading will cause conflict with another aircraft. The pilot should

options.a: always change altitude as required to avoid the other aircraft.
options.b: maintain the specified heading to comply with the CARs.
options.c: alter heading to avoid the other aircraft and advise ATC.
options.d: maintain the specified heading as separation will be provided by the controller.

questions.question: Unless ATC instructs otherwise, pilots operating VFR shall select transponder code 1200 when flying at or below ..... feet ASL and code ..... when flying above that altitude.
Pilots shall activate the transponder “ident” feature

options.a: before entering control zones.
options.b: only when so instructed by ATC.
options.c: before every change of altitude.
options.d: after every change of an assigned code.

questions.question: The holder of a student pilot permit may for the sole purpose of the holder’s own flight training act as PIC of an aircraft

options.a: only when accompanied by a flight instructor.
options.b: by day and night and authorized by a supervising flight instructor.
options.c: by day only and authorized by a supervising flight instructor.
options.d: while carrying passengers.

questions.question: Before setting out on any VFR flight, a pilot is required to

options.a: read all weather reports received from stations within 100 miles of destination.
options.b: file a flight itinerary.
options.c: be familiar with all available information appropriate to the flight.
options.d: obtain an ATC clearance.

questions.question: Terminal airspace dimensions and VHF sector frequencies for certain high density traffic airports in Canada are shown

options.a: in the Designated Airspace Handbook and the TC AIM.
options.b: on the VTA chart and in the CFS.
options.c: on the VTA and VNC charts.
options.d: in the CFS and on the VNC chart.

questions.question: Avoiding wake turbulence is

options.a: the sole responsibility of ATC.
options.b: the responsibility of the pilot, only when advised by ATC of the possibility of wake turbulence.
options.c: a responsibility shared by both the pilot and ATC.
options.d: the sole responsibility of the pilot.

questions.question: Hazardous wake turbulence caused by aircraft in still air

options.a: dissipates immediately.
options.b: dissipates rapidly.
options.c: may persist for 5 minutes or more.
options.d: persists indefinitely.

questions.question: Which response is most correct with respect to wake turbulence?

options.a: Wing tip vortices are carried by the ambient wind.
options.b: Wing tip vortices have a circular and downward motion.
options.c: Wake turbulence exists behind all aeroplanes and helicopters in flight.
options.d: All of the above are correct.

questions.question: The wing tip vortices generated by a heavy aeroplane can cause a lighter aircraft encountering them to

options.a: go out of control.
options.b: continue descent even when maximum power is applied.
options.c: sustain structural damage.
options.d: experience any of the above situations.

questions.question: During the two minutes after the passage of a heavy aeroplane in cruising flight, hazardous wing tip vortices will

options.a: dissipate completely.
options.b: dissipate rapidly.
options.c: dissipate very slowly.
options.d: remain at cruising altitude.

questions.question: The pilot of a light aircraft on final approach close behind a heavier aircraft should plan the approach to land

options.a: beyond the touchdown point of the other aircraft.
options.b: prior to the touchdown point of the other aircraft.
options.c: at the touchdown point of the other aircraft.
options.d: to the right or left of the touchdown point of the other aircraft.

questions.question: Wake turbulence is produced by

options.a: heavy aeroplanes only, regardless of their speed.
options.b: turbo-jet powered aircraft only.
options.c: fast moving aeroplanes only, regardless of their weight.
options.d: all fixed and rotary wing aircraft.

questions.question: Wake turbulence caused by a departing large aeroplane begins

options.a: before rotation.
options.b: with rotation.
options.c: after becoming airborne.
options.d: with full power application.

questions.question: Wake turbulence caused by a departing aeroplane is most severe immediately

options.a: before rotation.
options.b: following take-off.
options.c: above its flight path.
options.d: following full power application.

questions.question: Which statement concerning wing tip vortices is false?

options.a: Vortices normally settle below and behind the aircraft.
options.b: With a light crosswind, one vortex can remain stationary over the ground for some time.

questions.question: Wake turbulence will be greatest when generated by an aeroplane which is

options.a: heavy, landing configuration and slow speed.
options.b: heavy, clean configuration and slow speed.
options.c: light, clean configuration and high speed.
options.d: heavy, take-off configuration and slow speed.

questions.question: A helicopter in forward flight produces hazardous vortices

options.a: which rise above the helicopter.
options.b: similar to wing tip vortices.
options.c: which remains at the same level as the helicopter.
options.d: ahead of the helicopter.

questions.question: Which statement concerning vortices caused by helicopters is correct?

options.a: Helicopter vortices are generally weak and dissipate rapidly.
options.b: The size and weight of the helicopter has a direct influence on the intensity of the vortices.
options.c: Helicopter vortices are less intense than the vortices of an aeroplane of the same weight.
options.d: Wind does not influence the movement of vortices generated by a helicopter in hovering flight.

questions.question: What effect would a light crosswind have on the wing tip vortices generated by a large aeroplane that had just taken off? A light crosswind

options.a: could cause one vortex to remain over the runway for some time.
options.b: would rapidly dissipate the strength of both vortices.
options.c: would rapidly clear the runway of all vortices.
options.d: would not affect the lateral movement of the vortices.

questions.question: A flight crew member aware of being under a physical disability that might invalidate licence issue or renewal shall

options.a: so advise the Minister.
options.b: not commence a flight as a crew member.
options.c: forward the licence to the Regional Aviation Medical Officer.
options.d: fly as crew member only if a back-up member is available.

questions.question: What is the recommended treatment for hyperventilation below 8,000 feet?

options.a: Increase the depth of breathing.
options.b: Hold the breath and perform a Valsalva manoeuvre.
options.c: Slow the breathing rate to below 12 times per minute.
options.d: Increase oxygen flow rates.

questions.question: Damage to the ear drum in flight is most likely to occur

options.a: during a climb.
options.b: during a descent.
options.c: when using supplementary oxygen.
options.d: after SCUBA diving.

questions.question: Clearing the ears on a rapid descent may be assisted by

options.a: swallowing.
options.b: opening the mouth widely or yawning.
options.c: a Valsalva manoeuvre.
options.d: all of the above.

questions.question: Flight crew members who require decompression stops on the way to the surface when SCUBA diving should not fly for

options.a: 4 hours.
options.b: 8 hours.
options.c: 12 hours.
options.d: 24 hours.

questions.question: With regard to fatigue, which statement is correct according to the information given under the “Medical Information” section of the TC AIM?

options.a: Financial or family problems do not influence tolerance to fatigue.
options.b: Fatigue slows reaction time and causes foolish inattentive errors.
options.c: A fatigued person recuperates more quickly as altitude is gained.
options.d: A fatigued person must have food immediately before and during flight.

questions.question: A pilot who has donated blood should not act as a flight crew member for at least the next

options.a: 12 hours.
options.b: 24 hours.
options.c: 36 hours.
options.d: 48 hours.

questions.question: Any pilot who has had a general anaesthetic should not act as a flight crew member

options.a: during the next 12 hrs.
options.b: during the next 36 hrs.
options.c: during the next 48 hrs.
options.d: unless advised it is safe to do so by a doctor.

questions.question: Any pilot who has had a local anaesthetic for extensive dental procedures should not act as a flight crew member during the next

options.a: 12 hrs.
options.b: 24 hrs.
options.c: 36 hrs.
options.d: 48 hrs.

questions.question: Relatively small amounts of alcohol affect tolerance to hypoxia (lack of sufficient oxygen). This tolerance

options.a: deteriorates with increase of altitude.
options.b: improves with increase of altitude.
options.c: is not affected by altitude change.
options.d: remains constant to 6,000 feet ASL.

questions.question: Many common drugs such as cold tablets, cough mixtures, antihistamines and other over-the- counter remedies may seriously impair the judgement and co-ordination needed while flying. The safest rule is to

options.a: read the manufacturer’s warning to ensure that you are aware of possible reactions to such drugs.
options.b: take no medicine when you plan to fly, except on the advice of a Civil Aviation Medical Examiner…
options.c: allow at least 12 hours between taking any medicine or drugs and flying.
options.d: allow at least 8 hours between taking any medicine or drugs and flying.

questions.question: The Canadian Medical Certificate of a private pilot 40 years old and over is valid, in Canada, for a period of

options.a: 12 months.
options.b: 24 months.
options.c: 36 months.
options.d: 48 months.

questions.question: The Canadian Medical Certificate of a private pilot under 40 years of age is valid, in Canada, for a period of

options.a: 72 months.
options.b: 60 months.
options.c: 48 months.
options.d: 24 months.

questions.question: The amount of fuel and oil carried on board any helicopter at the commencement of a day VFR flight must be sufficient, to provide for foreseeable delays having been considered, to fly to the destination aerodrome,

options.a: and thereafter for 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.b: then to a specified alternate and thereafter for 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.c: and thereafter for 20 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.d: then to a specified alternate and thereafter for 20 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.question: The amount of fuel carried on board any propeller-driven aeroplane at the commencement of a day VFR flight must be sufficient, having regard to the meteorological conditions and foreseeable delays that are expected in flight, to fly to the destination aerodrome

options.a: and then fly for a period of 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.b: and then fly for a period of 30 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.c: then to a specified alternate aerodrome and then for a period of 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
options.d: then to a specified alternate aerodrome and then fly for a period of 30 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.question: If a flight plan is not filed, a flight itinerary must be filed

options.a: for flights proceeding 25 NM or more from the point of origin.
options.b: only for flights in sparsely settled areas.
options.c: for flights destined to land at aerodromes or places other than the point of origin.
options.d: for all flights.

questions.question: After landing from a VFR flight for which a flight plan has been filed, the pilot shall report the arrival to the appropriate ATS unit within

options.a: 15 minutes.
options.b: 30 minutes.
options.c: 45 minutes.
options.d: 60 minutes.

questions.question: When there is a deviation from a VFR flight plan, ATC shall be notified of such deviation

options.a: as soon as possible.
options.b: within 10 minutes.
options.c: within 30 minutes.
options.d: within 60 minutes after landing.

questions.question: Where no search and rescue initiation time is specified in a flight itinerary, when shall the pilot report to the “responsible person”?

options.a: Within one hour after the expiration of the estimated duration of the flight specified in the flight itinerary.
options.b: Within one hour after landing.
options.c: Within 24 hours after the expiration of the estimated duration of the flight specified in the flight itinerary.
options.d: As soon as practicable after landing but no later than 24 hours after the last reported ETA.

questions.question: With regard to a flight itinerary, the “responsible person” means an individual who

options.a: has agreed to report the aircraft overdue.
options.b: is 18 years of age or over.
options.c: holds an aeronautical licence.
options.d: has agreed to report the arrival of the aircraft.

questions.question: Where a VFR flight plan has been filed, an arrival report must be filed by the pilot

options.a: by advising an ATS unit.
options.b: at each intermediate stop and then reopened on take-off.
options.c: by parking the aircraft in close proximity to the tower.
options.d: except at airports served by a control tower in which case the controller will automatically file the arrival report.

questions.question: Estimated elapsed time A to B                       1 hour 15 minutes. Estimated stopover time at B                       30 minutes. Estimated elapsed time B to C                      1 hour 20 minutes. Using the above information, what time should be entered in the “Elapsed Time” box of a VFR flight plan?

options.a: 3 hours 50 minutes.
options.b: 3 hours 20 minutes.
options.c: 3 hours 05 minutes.
options.d: 2 hours 35 minutes.

questions.question: When filing a VFR flight plan with an intermediate stop, the total elapsed time to be entered is the total

options.a: elapsed time for all legs including the duration of the intermediate stop.
options.b: elapsed time for all legs, plus the intermediate stop, plus 45 minutes.
options.c: flight time for all legs.
options.d: elapsed time to the first landing plus intermediate stops.

questions.question: How is an intermediate stop indicated on the flight plan form for a VFR flight?

options.a: By including duration of the intermediate stop in ‘Elapsed Time’ box as ATC automatically checks time between points.
options.b: Same as any VFR flight plan if the intermediate time does not exceed 30 minutes at each point.
options.c: By repeating the name of intermediate stop and its duration in the ‘Route’ column.
options.d: By simply indicating “Intermediate Stop” in “Other Information” column.

questions.question: An ATC instruction

options.a: must be complied with when received by the pilot providing the safety of the aircraft is not jeopardized.
options.b: must be ‘read back’ in full to the controller and confirmed before becoming effective.
options.c: is in effect advice provided by ATC and does not require acceptance or formal acknowledgement by the pilot concerned.
options.d: is the same as an ATC clearance.

questions.question: An ATC clearance

options.a: is the same as an ATC instruction.
options.b: is advice provided by ATC and does not require acceptance or acknowledgement by the PIC.
options.c: requires compliance when accepted by the PIC.
options.d: must be complied with when received by the PIC.

questions.question: A pilot, after accepting a clearance and subsequently finding that all or part of the clearance cannot be complied with, should

options.a: disregard the clearance.
options.b: comply with only the part that is suitable.
options.c: comply as best as possible under the circumstances to carry out the clearance and need not say anything to ATC.
options.d: comply as best as possible under the circumstances and advise ATC as soon as possible.

questions.question: After accepting a clearance and subsequently finding that it cannot be complied with, a pilot should

options.a: take any immediate action required and advise ATC as soon as possible.
options.b: comply as best as possible under the circumstances and say nothing to ATC.
options.c: disregard the clearance.
options.d: comply with the suitable parts.

questions.question: An ATC clearance or instruction is predicated on known traffic only. Therefore, when a pilot is proceeding in accordance with a clearance or instruction

options.a: ATC is relieved of the responsibility for traffic separation.
options.b: the responsibility for traffic separation is divided between ATC and the pilot.
options.c: the pilot is not relieved of the responsibility for traffic avoidance.
options.d: the pilot is relieved of the responsibility for traffic avoidance.

questions.question: If all or part of an ATC clearance is unacceptable, a pilot should

options.a: comply as best as possible under the circumstances.
options.b: refuse the clearance without giving a reason for refusal.
options.c: acknowledge the clearance and read back only the acceptable parts.
options.d: refuse the clearance and inform ATC of their intentions.

questions.question: In an emergency requiring the use of an ELT, it should be turned on

options.a: immediately and left on.
options.b: at the ETA in the flight plan.
options.c: for the first five minutes of each hour UTC.
options.d: during daylight hours only to conserve the battery.

questions.question: An aircraft’s 121.5 MHz ELT may be switched to transmit for test purposes anytime

options.a: following a hard landing.
options.b: during the first 5 minutes of any hour UTC.
options.c: following a component or battery change.
options.d: prior to flight and listening on 121.5 MHz.

questions.question: Before shutting down you can verify that the aircraft’s ELT is not transmitting by

options.a: checking that the ELT switch is in the off position.
options.b: listening on 121.5 MHz for a signal.
options.c: ensuring that the master switch is off.
options.d: checking the ELT visual warning light.

questions.question: All accidental ELT activations should be reported to the

options.a: airport manager.
options.b: R.C.M.P.
options.c: Minister.
options.d: nearest ATS unit.

questions.question: When an aircraft engine is left running on the ground and no person remains onboard, the aircraft’s movement must be restricted and

options.a: it must remain in sight of the pilot at all times.
options.b: it must not be left unattended.
options.c: its gross weight must be below 4,409 LB (2,000 kg).
options.d: its control locks must be installed.

questions.question: When confronted with an approaching thunderstorm, a take-off or landing

options.a: should be avoided as a sudden wind shift or low level turbulence could cause a loss of control.
options.b: is safe if you can see under the thunderstorm through to the other side.
options.c: should be avoided unless the take-off can be made away from the thunderstorm.
options.d: is safe if the thunderstorm is regarded as light.

questions.question: An isolated thunderstorm is in close proximity to your aerodrome of intended landing. You should

options.a: land giving due consideration to wind shear on final approach.
options.b: hold over a known point clear of the thunderstorm until it is well past the aerodrome.
options.c: land as quickly as possible.
options.d: add one-half the wind gust factor to the recommended landing speed and land.

questions.question: The take-off thrust blast danger area includes at least that area extending back from the tail of a medium size jet transport aeroplane for

options.a: 1,200 feet.
options.b: 900 feet.
options.c: 500 feet.
options.d: 450 feet.

questions.question: The ground idle blast danger area extends back from the tail of a jumbo jet aeroplane for at least

options.a: 200 feet.
options.b: 450 feet.
options.c: 600 feet.
options.d: 750 feet.

questions.question: The ground idle blast danger area extends back from the tail of a medium size jet aeroplane for at least

options.a: 200 feet.
options.b: 450 feet.
options.c: 600 feet.
options.d: 750 feet.

questions.question: The ground idle blast danger area extends back from the tail of an executive jet aeroplane for

options.a: 200 feet.
options.b: 450 feet.
options.c: 600 feet.
options.d: 750 feet.

questions.question: A 45 kt blast area can be expected ..... behind the propellers of a large turbo-prop aeroplane during taxi.

options.a: 60 feet.
options.b: 80 feet.
options.c: 100 feet.
options.d: 120 feet.

questions.question: ATC advises that simultaneous operations are in progress at an airport. Pilots could expect a clearance to

options.a: take-off over top of an aircraft on an intersecting runway.
options.b: take-off on a specified parallel runway.
options.c: land and hold short of an intersecting runway.
options.d: land on a specified parallel runway.

questions.question: When issued a clearance to land and hold short of an intersecting runway, pilots

options.a: shall comply regardless of the circumstances.
options.b: may taxi across the intersection after the departing or arriving aircraft has cleared their path.
options.c: who inadvertently go through the intersection should immediately do a 180° turn and backtrack to the hold position.
options.d: should immediately inform ATC if they are unable to comply.

questions.question: ADIZ rules normally apply

options.a: only to aircraft flying above 12,500 feet.
options.b: only to aircraft flying at true airspeeds of 180 kt or more.
options.c: only to all southbound aircraft.
options.d: to all aircraft.

questions.question: When operating in accordance with VFR, aircraft shall be flown

options.a: clear of aerodrome traffic zones.
options.b: clear of control zones.
options.c: with visual reference to the surface.
options.d: in compliance with all of the above.

questions.question: Normally, a helicopter in uncontrolled airspace at less than 1,000 feet AGL may operate during the day in flight visibility which is not less than

options.a: ½ mile.
options.b: 1 mile.
options.c: 2 miles.
options.d: 3 miles.

questions.question: What distance from cloud shall an aircraft maintain when flying below 1,000 feet AGL within uncontrolled airspace?

options.a: At least 2,000 ft horizontally and 500 ft vertically.
options.b: At least 1 mile horizontally and 500 ft vertically.
options.c: At least 2 miles horizontally and 500 ft vertically.
options.d: Clear of cloud.

questions.question: No person shall drop anything from an aircraft in flight

options.a: which will create a hazard to persons or property.
options.b: unless approval has been granted by the Minister.
options.c: unless over an authorized jettison area.
options.d: unless it is attached to a parachute.

questions.question: A person may conduct aerobatic manoeuvres in an aircraft

options.a: over an airport provided the appropriate frequency is monitored.
options.b: over the suburban area of a city above 2,000 feet AGL.
options.c: within Class F advisory airspace when visibility is 3 miles or greater.
options.d: within Class C airspace when the visibility is 1 mile or greater.

questions.question: CARs state that after the consumption of any alcoholic beverage, no person shall act as a crew member of an aircraft within

options.a: 12 hours.
options.b: 24hours.
options.c: 36 hours.
options.d: 48 hours.

questions.question: ‘Day’ in Canada is that period of time between

options.a: sunrise and sunset.
options.b: one hour before sunrise and one hour after sunset.
options.c: the beginning of morning civil twilight and the end of evening civil twilight.
options.d: the end of morning civil twilight and the beginning of evening civil twilight.

questions.question: ‘Night’ in Canada is that period of time between

options.a: sunset and sunrise.
options.b: the beginning of evening civil twilight and the end of morning civil twilight.
options.c: one hour after sunset and one hour before sunrise.
options.d: the end of evening civil twilight and the beginning of morning civil twilight.

questions.question: Formation flying is permitted only if such flights

options.a: have been pre-arranged by the pilots-in-command.
options.b: are conducted above 3,000 feet AGL.
options.c: are conducted by commercial pilots.
options.d: are led by a pilot whose licence is endorsed for formation flight.

questions.question: Flight through active Class F airspace with the designator CYR

options.a: may be undertaken only by aircraft equipped with two-way radio communication and a transponder.
options.b: is restricted to military aircraft operating under the authority of the Minister of National Defence.
options.c: will be approved only for aircraft on IFR flight plans under positive radar control.
options.d: is permitted only in accordance with permission issued by the user agency.

questions.question: Which statement is correct with regard to “advisory airspace”?

options.a: A transient aircraft entering active advisory airspace shall be equipped with a serviceable transponder.
options.b: Non-participating VFR aircraft are encouraged to avoid flight in advisory airspace during active periods specified on aeronautical charts and NOTAM.
options.c: Aircraft need to be equipped with a two-way radio to enter active advisory airspace.
options.d: Only military aircraft may enter advisory airspace depicted on aeronautical charts.

questions.question: Except as provided by CARs, unless taking off, landing or attempting to land, no person shall fly a helicopter over a built-up area or open air assembly of persons except at an altitude that will permit, in the event of an emergency, the landing of the aircraft without creating a hazard to persons or property on the surface, and such altitude shall not be less than ..... above the highest obstacle within a horizontal radius of ..... from the aircraft.

options.a: 3,000 ft, 1 mile.

questions.question: Over non-populous areas or over open water, a pilot must maintain a minimum distance of ..... feet from any person, vessel, vehicle or structure.

Except for balloons and as provided by CARs, no person shall cause any aircraft to take off or attempt to take off from, land on or attempt to land on, any surface within the built-up area of any city or town unless

options.a: the aircraft is multi-engined.
options.b: all obstacles on approach and departure can be cleared by a minimum of 500 ft.
options.c: that surface is an airport or military aerodrome.
options.d: noise abatement procedures are followed.

questions.question: What is the height AGL above which an aircraft in VFR flight shall be operated to conform with the Cruising Altitudes Order?

options.a: 700 feet.
options.b: 2,200 feet.
options.c: 3,000 feet.
options.d: 3,500 feet.

questions.question: An aircraft cruising VFR in level flight above 3,000 feet AGL on a track of 290°M shall be flown at an

options.a: even thousand foot altitude.
options.b: even thousand plus 500 foot altitude.
options.c: odd thousand foot altitude.
options.d: odd thousand plus 500 foot altitude.

questions.question: The selection of a cruising altitude in the southern domestic airspace should be based on the

options.a: true track.
options.b: magnetic track.
options.c: true heading.
options.d: magnetic heading.

questions.question: Every person who is the holder of any pilot licence or permit shall, on demand, produce such licence or permit for inspection by persons authorized by the Minister, by peace officers and

options.a: FSS operators.
options.b: Transport Canada airport managers.
options.c: immigration officers.
options.d: all of the above.

questions.question: Low Level Airspace is defined as, all airspace

options.a: extending upwards from 2,200 feet AGL within designated airways.
options.b: extending upwards from 1000 feet AGL within designated airways.
options.c: extending upwards from the surface of the earth within designated airways.
options.d: within the Canadian Domestic Airspace below 18,000 feet ASL.

questions.question: A Control Zone normally is controlled airspace extending upwards from

options.a: 2,200 feet above the surface of the earth.
options.b: 700 feet above the surface of the earth.
options.c: the surface of the earth to 3,000 feet.
options.d: a specified height above the surface of the earth.

questions.question: ‘Controlled Airspace’ means all airspace of defined dimensions within which

options.a: Control Zone regulations are in force.
options.b: security regulations are in force.
options.c: Special VFR flight only is permitted.
options.d: an ATC service is provided.

questions.question: When in VFR flight within controlled airspace, a pilot must remain clear of cloud by at least

options.a: 500 feet vertically and 1 mile horizontally.
options.b: 500 feet vertically and 2,000 feet horizontally.
options.c: 1,000 feet vertically and 1 mile horizontally.
options.d: 1,000 feet vertically and 3 miles horizontally.

questions.question: The minimum flight visibility for VFR flight within a low level airway is

options.a: 1 mile.
options.b: 1½ miles.
options.c: 2 miles.
options.d: 3 miles.

questions.question: When in VFR flight within a Control Zone, a pilot must remain clear of cloud by at least

options.a: 500 feet vertically and 2,000 feet horizontally.
options.b: 500 feet vertically and 1 mile horizontally.
options.c: 1,000 feet vertically and 1 mile horizontally.
options.d: 1,000 feet vertically and 3 miles horizontally.

questions.question: VFR cross-country pilots wishing to cross through any part of a Class C Control Zone should

options.a: advise the associated FSS.
options.b: monitor the Approach Control frequency.
options.c: advise ATC of their intentions and obtain a clearance.
options.d: conform with circuit direction at that airport.

questions.question: ATC may authorize an aircraft equipped with a functioning two-way radio to transit a Control Zone under day SVFR provided the flight visibility and, when reported, ground visibility, are each not less than

options.a: ½ mile.
options.b: 1 mile.
options.c: 2 miles.
options.d: 3 miles.

questions.question: ATC may authorize a helicopter equipped with a functioning two-way radio to transit a Control Zone under day SVFR where the flight visibility and, when reported, ground visibility are each not less than

options.a: 1 mile and operated at not less than 500 feet AGL.
options.b: ½ mile.
options.c: 1 mile.
options.d: ½ mile and operated at not less than 500 feet AGL.

questions.question: An aircraft flying in accordance with Special VFR would be flying within

options.a: a Control Zone.
options.b: an Aerodrome Traffic Zone.
options.c: a Terminal Control Area.
options.d: an airway.

questions.question: An arriving VFR flight shall make initial radio contact with the control tower

options.a: upon entering an Aerodrome Traffic Zone.
options.b: prior to entering a Control Zone.
options.c: immediately prior to joining the circuit.
options.d: immediately after entering a Control Zone.

questions.question: VFR flight within Class B airspace is permitted

options.a: only when the flight visibility is 5 miles or better.
options.b: for all aircraft except gliders and balloons.
options.c: if the pilot holds a Class B Airspace Endorsement.
options.d: in accordance with an ATC clearance.

questions.question: The pilot of an arriving VFR flight shall make initial radio contact with a control tower in Class C airspace

options.a: immediately after entering the Control Zone.
options.b: 10 NM outside the Control Zone.
options.c: prior to entering the Control Zone.
options.d: immediately prior to joining the circuit.

questions.question: Unless otherwise authorized, a pilot on a VFR flight operating within a Class C terminal control area must

options.a: exit the airspace whenever the weather deteriorates below VFR limits.
options.b: establish radio contact with the appropriate ATC unit only when transiting the associated control zone.
options.c: establish and maintain radio communication with the appropriate ATC Unit.
options.d: contact radar service only when taking off or landing at the major airport concerned.

questions.question: The primary objective of an aviation safety investigation into an aircraft accident or aircraft incident is to

options.a: apportion blame and liability.
options.b: determine the adequacy of insurance regulations.
options.c: enforce regulations.
options.d: prevent recurrences.

questions.question: Details on civil aviation accident reporting procedures can be found in the

options.a: TC AIM
options.b: Canadian Aviation Regulations.
options.c: Canada Flight Supplement.
options.d: Aviation Safety Manual.

questions.question: When an aircraft accident occurs, the pilot or operator of the aircraft involved shall ensure that the particulars of the accident are reported to the TSB

options.a: within 7 days by registered mail.
options.b: within 24 hours by telephone.
options.c: within 48 hours by facsimile.
options.d: as soon as possible and by the quickest means available.

questions.question: The TSB shall be notified of a reportable aviation accident when

options.a: a person sustains serious or fatal injury as a result of being in or coming into direct contact with any part of an aircraft.
options.b: an aircraft sustains damage or structural failure adversely affecting performance or flight characteristics and requiring major repair or replacement.
options.c: an aircraft is missing or completely inaccessible.
options.d: any of the above conditions exist.

questions.question: The TSB considers missing aircraft to be

options.a: a reportable aviation incident.
options.b: an occurrence which need not be reported.
options.c: an aviation incident which need not be reported.
options.d: a reportable aviation accident.

questions.question: When two aircraft are approaching head-on or approximately so and there is danger of collision, each shall

options.a: alter heading to the right.
options.b: alter heading to the left.
options.c: avoid the other by changing altitude.
options.d: turn on the anti-collision light.

questions.question: When two aircraft are on converging headings at approximately the same altitude, the aircraft that has the other on its right shall give way except that, gliders shall give way to

options.a: rotary wing aircraft.
options.b: "heavy" aeroplanes.
options.c: airships.
options.d: balloons.

questions.question: "Controlled Airspace" means an airspace of defined dimensions within which

options.a: aircraft must proceed in accordance with a SCATANA clearance.
options.b: an Air Traffic Control (ATC) service is provided.
options.c: only aircraft equipped with functioning two-way radio may operate.
options.d: Control is provided for instrument flight rules (IFR) traffic only.

questions.question: Except for ultra-light aeroplanes, no person shall fly an aircraft in Canada unless

options.a: it is registered.
options.b: there is in force with respect to the aircraft a Certificate of Airworthiness or a Flight Permit.
options.c: its nationality and registration marks are affixed to the aircraft in a proper manner, and are clear and visible.
options.d: all of the above conditions are met.

questions.question: No person shall attempt to fly as a flight crew member of a glider if

options.a: less than five take-offs and landings have been completed in the preceding six months.
options.b: within the preceding 14 hours alcohol has been consumed.
options.c: experiencing a physical disability likely to prohibit immediate licence renewal.
options.d: less than five supervised take-offs and landings have been completed in the preceding six months

questions.question: Except when taking off from or landing at an airport or military aerodrome aircraft shall not be flown over the built-up areas of any city, town or other settlement or over an open-air assembly of persons except at an altitude that will permit in the event of an emergency, the landing of the aircraft without undue hazard to persons or to property on the surface; such altitude shall not in any case be less than . . . . . above the highest obstacle within a horizontal radius of two thousand feet from the aircraft. The correct entry to complete the blank space is

options.a: 500 feet.
options.b: 1,000 feet.
options.c: 2,000 feet.
options.d: 3,000 feet.

questions.question: A steady green light directed at an aircraft in flight means

options.a: cleared to land.
options.b: return for landing.
options.c: cleared to base leg.
options.d: cleared for final.

questions.question: In radio communications the signal which means that grave and imminent danger threatens and immediate assistance is required is the spoken phrase

options.a: SECURITE, SECURITE.
options.b: MAYDAY, MAYDAY, MAYDAY.
options.c: PAN PAN, PAN PAN, PAN PAN.
options.d: ALERT, ALERT.

questions.question: A glider is preparing for take-off from an aerodrome in the altimeter setting region. If the current altimeter setting is not available the altimeter shall be set to

options.a: the mean sea level pressure of the aerodrome.
options.b: the standard pressure setting.
options.c: the elevation of the aerodrome.
options.d: zero.

questions.question: The minimum flight visibility for VFR flight in a Control Area is

options.a: 6 miles.
options.b: 3 miles.
options.c: 2 miles.
options.d: 1 mile.

questions.question: Every owner of an aircraft shall preserve the Aircraft Journey Log for

options.a: one year after the date of the last entry in the log.
options.b: three years after the date of the last entry log.
options.c: as long as the aircraft has a valid Certificate of Airworthiness.
options.d: the life of the aircraft.

questions.question: An Air Traffic Control "instruction"

options.a: is in effect "advice" provided by ATC and does not require acknowledgement by the pilot concerned.
options.b: is the same as an ATC "clearance" when issued by a Flight Service Section.
options.c: requires compliance when received unless aircraft safety is jeopardized.
options.d: must be "read back" in full to the controller and confirmed before becoming effective.

questions.question: Pilots are warned of the dangerous turbulence existing in the wake of large aeroplane in flight. The turbulence referred to is caused by

options.a: expanding wing tip vortices.
options.b: propeller or jet wash.
options.c: air displaced by the fuselage.
options.d: the vacuum created by aircraft passage.

questions.question: No person shall walk, drive or park any vehicle on any part of an airport used for the movement of aircraft, except in accordance with permission given by the appropriate ATC unit or, in the absence of any such unit, by

options.a: an airport guide or commissionnaire.
options.b: the local flying club or school manager.
options.c: the operator of the airport.
options.d: the local police or RCMP.

questions.question: Pilots should be aware that an early symptom of hypoxia (lack of oxygen) is a sense of

options.a: well-being.
options.b: drowsiness.
options.c: dizziness.
options.d: panic.

questions.question: ‘Day’ in Canada is defined as that period of time between

options.a: sunrise and sunset.
options.b: one hour before sunrise and one hour after sunset.
options.c: the end of morning civil twilight and the beginning of evening civil twilight.
options.d: the beginning of morning civil twilight and the end of evening civil twilight.

questions.question: Unless authorized by ATC, no person shall fly a glider within Class C airspace unless

options.a: the pilot receives and acknowledges a VFR clearance to do so.
options.b: the ground visibility is at least 3 miles.
options.c: the pilot maintains a listening watch on an appropriate radio frequency.
options.d: all of the above conditions are met.

questions.question: The filing of a flight plan or itinerary is only required when a glider pilot plans to proceed

options.a: on an aerobatic flight.
options.b: to an altitude of more than 5,000 feet.
options.c: on a passenger carrying flight.
options.d: more than 25 NM from departure point.

questions.question: The pilot-in-command of a glider shall not fly at cabin pressure altitudes between 13,000 and 15,000 feet above MSL

options.a: unless continuously wearing and using oxygen masks supplying oxygen.
options.b: for more than 30 minutes, unless oxygen masks and a supply of oxygen is available.
options.c: unless passengers are continuously wearing and using oxygen masks supplying oxygen.
options.d: for more than 45 minutes, unless oxygen masks and a supply of oxygen is available.

questions.question: Loaded as above the

options.a: C of G is at the aft limit.
options.b: C of G is at the forward limit.
options.c: gross weight is exceeded
options.d: gross weight is 715 LB.

questions.question: The total lift force acting on an aerofoil in flight is considered to act

options.a: upwards with reference to the earth’s surface.
options.b: at 90º to the aircraft longitudinal axis.
options.c: at 90º to the average relative air flow.
options.d: at 90º to the average wing chord.

questions.question: At the point of stall an increase in the angle of attack results in

options.a: more lift and more drag.
options.b: more lift and less drag.
options.c: less lift and more drag.
options.d: no effect on lift or drag.

questions.question: Longitudinal stability refers to stability about the

options.a: longitudinal axis.
options.b: lateral axis.
options.c: normal axis.
options.d: directional axis.

questions.question: The stalling speed of a glider is

options.a: the same in coordinated turns as in straight and level flight.
options.b: inversely proportional to the wing loading.
options.c: not affected by aerodynamic loads.
options.d: greater in coordinated turns than in straight and level flight

questions.question: The ratio of the total lift force acting on the wings of a glider during flight to the gross weight of the glider is known as the

options.a: lift/drag ratio.
options.b: aspect ration.
options.c: power factor.
options.d: load factor.

questions.question: In straight and level flight at 6,000 feet ASL a glider stalls at 35 KT IAS. At what IAS will it stall in similar circumstances in the circuit at 1,000 feet ASL?

options.a: 27 KT.
options.b: 32 KT.
options.c: 35 KT.
options.d: More than 35 KT.

questions.question: While on tow a glider pilot is unable to release the tow rope. The signal to indicate this condition to the tow pilot is to

options.a: yaw the glider to the right and left in a series of skidding turns.
options.b: commence a series of "S" turns at normal tow position.
options.c: porpoise rapidly between high and low tow positions.
options.d: fly to the left of the tug and rock the glider’s wings.

questions.question: A "weak link" would most likely be associated with a

options.a: tow line.
options.b: control cable.
options.c: dive brake.
options.d: canopy latch.

questions.question: At a gliding ratio of 15:1, a glider descending 1,000 feet in still air will move forward a distance closest to
The above signal means

options.a: number two for take off.
options.b: take up slack.
options.c: stop.
options.d: begin take-off.

questions.question: In gliding, an important use of wing spoilers is to

options.a: increase the rate of descent at the same airspeed.
options.b: increase lift without increasing drag.
options.c: make safe approaches possible at slower airspeeds.
options.d: improve the lift/drag ratio.

questions.question: To maintain an IAS of 50 KT during an approach through a wind gradient where the head wind is decreasing, the pilot should

options.a: pitch the nose down.
options.b: pitch the nose up.
options.c: gradually apply spoilers.
options.d: maintain a constant pitch attitude.

questions.question: The flight manual states that the maximum cross-wind landing component applicable to a particular glider is 090/10 KT. Which wind velocity will permit landing within this limit when landing on runway 27?
When turning from downwind to base leg for landing, a strong wind could cause a pilot to experience an illusion of

options.a: skidding.
options.b: side-slipping.
options.c: overbanking.
options.d: increasing ground speed.

questions.question: A straight line drawn between any two points on this chart most closely approximates a

options.a: great circle.
options.b: rhumb line.
options.c: parallel of latitude.
options.d: meridian.

questions.question: The elevation of Carp Airport (N4519', W7601') is

options.a: 350 feet.
options.b: 382 feet.
options.c: 712 feet.
options.d: 1,062 feet.

questions.question: An aircraft in flight at an altitude of 2500 ASL feet over Carp Airport would be flying

options.a: outside of controlled airspace.
options.b: in the Ottawa Control Zone.
options.c: in controlled airspace.
options.d: above an Aerodrome Traffic Zone.

questions.question: The object of "swinging" a compass is to minimize error and record the

options.a: variation on various headings.
options.b: deviation on various headings.
options.c: turning error on all headings.
options.d: acceleration errors on various headings.

questions.question: When you put your aircraft away at night the altimeter set to the elevation of the airport reads 520 feet. During the night a low pressure area moves over the airport and the atmospheric pressure drops sharply. On checking the altimeter the next morning you would expect the indicated altitude to be

options.a: 520 feet.
options.b: higher than 520 feet.
options.c: lower than 520 feet.
options.d: the same as the current altimeter setting.

questions.question: The most important element in the atmosphere from the weather standpoint is

options.a: water droplets.
options.b: nitrogen.
options.c: water vapour.
options.d: oxygen.

questions.question: Clouds which form when moist warm air overruns cold air, are caused because the warm air

options.a: is cooled by the cold air underneath.
options.b: is cooled by the surrounding cold air aloft.
options.c: becomes unstable as a result of cooling from below.
options.d: cools as a result of expansion as it rises.

questions.question: Wind is caused by

options.a: the rotation of the earth.
options.b: friction between the air and the ground.
options.c: horizontal pressure differences.
options.d: the movements of fronts.

questions.question: In the northern hemisphere, the winds blow

options.a: direct from high to low pressure areas.
options.b: clockwise around a high and counter-clockwise around a low.
options.c: at a 30 angle out of a low pressure area.
options.d: at a 30 angle into a high pressure area.

questions.question: During a descent from 2,000 feet AGL to the surface you will usually find that the wind

options.a: veers and increases.
options.b: backs and increases.
options.c: veers and decreases.
options.d: backs and decreases.

questions.question: The lapse rate is the rate of change of

options.a: temperature with height.
options.b: pressure with height.
options.c: pressure in the horizontal.
options.d: temperature in the horizontal.

questions.question: Air masses which are being cooled from below are characterized by

options.a: strong winds, cumulus cloud, good visibility.
options.b: uniform temperature, good visibility.
options.c: decreasing humidity, poor visibility.
options.d: fog, poor visibility and layer cloud.

questions.question: The following sequence of clouds is observed at an airport: cirrus, altostratus, nimbostratus. The observer should expect

options.a: the passage of a cold front.
options.b: anticyclonic weather.
options.c: the passage of a warm front.
options.d: clearing skies and a decrease in temperature.

questions.question: Which clouds have the international family prefix "Cumulo"?

options.a: Clouds with bases between surface and 6,500 feet AGL.
options.b: Clouds with bases between 6,500 feet and 20,000 feet AGL.
options.c: Clouds with bases above 20,000 feet AGL.
options.d: Clouds of vertical development.

questions.question: Unless otherwise noted, cloud bases are forecast in height

options.a: ASL in both the Area Forecast and the TAF.
options.b: AGL in both the Area Forecast and the TAF.
options.c: AGL in Area Forecast and ASL in the TAF.
options.d: ASL in the Area Forecast and AGL in the TAF.

questions.question: Regarding cloud amount, the words "overcast" and "broken" mean respectively

options.a: 7/8 to 8/8 and 5/8 to 8/8 cloud.
options.b: 7/8 to 8/8 and 4/8 to 7/8 cloud.
options.c: 8/8 and 4/8 to 7/8 cloud.
options.d: 8/8 and 5/8 to 7/8 cloud.
